title: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
subtitle: The nurse supports a patient with PTSD — building safety and trust, helping ground them during a flashback, identifying triggers, and avoiding anything that re-traumatizes.

patient.name: PTSD
patient.admitDate: The nurse supports a patient with post-traumatic stress disorder.

nursesNote: The nurse supports a patient with post-traumatic stress disorder, a reaction that can follow exposure to a traumatic event, with symptoms lasting more than a month. Core symptoms fall into groups: intrusion (flashbacks, nightmares, and distressing memories that feel like reliving the event), avoidance (staying away from people, places, or reminders, with emotional numbing), negative changes in mood and thinking, and hyperarousal (hypervigilance, an exaggerated startle response, irritability, and poor sleep). The foundation of care is physical and emotional safety and trust: the nurse keeps the environment calm, predictable, and consistent, and explains actions before doing them. During a flashback the nurse stays calm, helps the patient ground to the here-and-now by orienting them (stating who and where they are, naming objects in the room) and guiding slow breathing, and reassures them they are safe. The nurse helps the patient identify personal triggers but does not force the patient to recount trauma details before they are ready, letting the patient lead. Sudden touch, approaching from behind, cornering, or loud, chaotic settings are avoided because they can re-traumatize. Effective treatment includes trauma-focused therapy (such as CBT or EMDR) and medications like SSRIs. The nurse also watches for co-occurring depression, substance use, and suicide risk, and connects the patient to ongoing support.
providerOrders: Establish physical and emotional safety and trust
Maintain a calm, predictable environment and explain actions first
Help the patient ground to the present during flashbacks
Help identify triggers without forcing trauma disclosure
Refer to trauma-focused therapy and monitor for depression, SUD, and suicide risk

opts.text: PTSD can follow exposure to a traumatic event
Flashbacks and nightmares are intrusion symptoms
Hypervigilance and exaggerated startle are common
Forcing the patient to retell the trauma helps most
Avoiding reminders is a core symptom
Safety and trust are the foundation of care
A calm, predictable environment helps
Grounding orients the patient to the present
Sudden touch and cornering are reassuring
Identifying triggers supports coping
Explaining actions before doing them builds trust
Co-occurring depression and SUD should be assessed

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order helping a patient having a flashback

opts.text: Ensure safety and stay calm
Speak gently and avoid sudden touch
Ground the patient to the here-and-now
Guide slow breathing and reassure safety
Discuss triggers and coping afterward
Vivid flashbacks of the event
Staying away from places that recall the trauma
Jumpy, easily startled, always on guard
Feeling emotionally numb and detached
Recurrent nightmares of the event
Refusing to talk about or near the trauma

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A loud noise triggers a flashback; the patient is terrified and disoriented
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Ensure safety and reduce the noise/stimulation
Approach calmly from the front, not behind
Orient the patient to who and where they are
Coach slow breathing and reassure safety
Debrief triggers and document afterward
Explaining procedures before starting them
Grabbing the patient's arm during a flashback
Helping the patient name objects in the room
Insisting they describe the trauma in detail now
Keeping the environment calm and predictable
Approaching quietly from behind to surprise them
Assessing for depression and suicide risk
Dismissing the flashback as overreacting

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Psychosocial Integrity, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
